What pass requires: The target standard is named consistently across all three tiered worksheets, and the tasks on each tier address that standard — no tier substitutes a different concept or skill. FIRST identify the structurally hardest case of the target standard yourself; verify it stays in play at EVERY tier — scaffolded at the Below tier, not dropped. State the hardest case in the explanation.

Below tier explicitly names a specific prerequisite standard or concept (by standard code or full name). Above tier explicitly names a specific forward standard or extension concept. Both must appear in the teacher plan and be traceable to the tier's design. A related concept is acceptable in place of a different standard — but it must be explicitly named, not implied. A vague reference ('students should have some background') does not pass.

No scaffold in any tier provides the answer directly: no fill-in-the-blank with only one possible word, no embedded answer key, no step-by-step guide requiring zero student decisions, and no keyword or shortcut strategies (math: "'together' means add"; ELA: "the central idea is always in the first sentence"; a sentence starter that hands the student the claim). At least one problem or task in each tier requires a student decision that is not resolved by the scaffold provided.

The differentiation between tiers is achieved by varying supports and cognitive demand, not by varying topic, text type, or learning channel (visual/auditory/kinesthetic). Below and At tiers address the same question in the same context; Above extends the same task.

Every tier works toward the SAME objective in the SAME scenario or text context. Task wording and supports MAY vary between tiers — judge the goal, not the phrasing. Differences must be limited to phrasing, added supports, visual aids, sentence frames, or a reduced number of tasks. Fail ONLY if a tier changes the goal, the context, or the kind of work students produce (e.g., one tier writes about a different topic, or answers multiple-choice while the others compose). Quote any tier where the goal or context itself changed.

Below-tier materials include at least one concrete representation or visual support — e.g., math: number line, tape diagram; ELA: story map, paragraph frame, text-evidence chart, annotation frame; science/social studies: labeled diagram, graphic organizer, image — on the same page or activity as a target-concept task, not relegated to a separate reference section.

Scaffolds are not uniformly present across all problems. Some problems have more support than others, creating a visible reduction in scaffold density within the tier or across the problem set (e.g., the last tasks in each tier carry less embedded support than the first).

Teacher plan states tier assignments explicitly, justifies how students are assigned to tiers (what evidence places a student in a tier), and includes language indicating groups are revisable based on formative evidence from this lesson (e.g., exit-ticket-driven regrouping) — not static ability tracks.

If the teacher has not confirmed using a specific curriculum, the output contains no references to curriculum-specific routine names, activity structures, or proprietary material names (e.g., no "IM centers," no "scientist circle," no "text-based answer protocol,"). Auto-pass if teacher confirmed curriculum use in the prompt.
Notes: Catches curriculum-specific routines leaking into outputs for teachers who never mentioned that curriculum.

What pass requires: All three tiers require students to do more than compute or recall: at least one task in each tier asks students to explain, justify, compare, or apply their understanding. No tier is limited to rote procedural steps — name any tier that is.

The problem context, real-world scenario, or narrative setting used in the At-tier worksheet appears — unchanged or closely parallel — in the Below-tier worksheet. Below tier does not receive a stripped-down or drill-only version of the task while At/Above receive the interesting version.
Notes: Avoids creating 'work' for the Below tier and 'fun' for the Above tier.

Criterion: Student agency prompt on each worksheet
What pass requires: Each tiered worksheet includes at least one open-ended or self-reflection prompt (e.g., "explain your thinking," "what strategy did you use?", "how confident are you?"). A 'show/explain how you know' prompt attached to a task counts. The prompt appears on all three tiers — name any tier missing it.

Criterion: Correct file structure, complete in one output turn
What pass requires: Output includes a single cohesive teacher-facing plan and three separate student-facing worksheets (Groups A, B, C — below / at / above tier), all delivered with substance in a single output turn once the build is underway. The in-chat draft, clarifying questions, and the turns that answer them precede the build and do not count against this criterion.

Teacher plan includes at least one mid-lesson check point more specific than "circulate and observe" — a check with a defined trigger, artifact, or decision — plus a tiered exit ticket whose sort buckets (Got it / Almost there / Needs re-teaching) carry explicit criteria distinguishing them.

Teacher plan includes an explicitly labeled anchor activity (e.g., "Early finishers:" or "Anchor activity:") that asks students to produce something — an explanation, a creation, a connection, or a question — rather than complete additional practice problems identical to the main worksheet.

Teacher plan includes at least two explicitly labeled rationale notes (e.g., "Why this works:", "Pedagogical note:") that each name a specific tier design choice and state a reason for it. Generic statements ("scaffolds help learners") do not count.

Differentiated materials include at least two of the following features applied across all tiers, not only the Below tier: sentence stems or frames, vocabulary glossary or word bank, diagram or visual representation, bilingual support, or multiple-format response option. Name the features and where they appear; features appearing only on the Below tier do not satisfy this.

Two-direction cross-read. Direction 1: every task or activity the teacher plan describes for students has a corresponding student-facing version in at least one worksheet (anchor activities included — if the plan says early finishers build or write something, a printed task for it must exist somewhere). Direction 2: every discrete task on each worksheet appears in the teacher plan's description of that tier. List every mismatch with the document and task quoted; pass only if BOTH directions are clean.
Notes: Independent check of teacher/student mismatch — a common failure mode.

What pass requires: Teacher plan closes with a labeled section (heading or inline label using terms such as 'Next Steps', 'Follow-up options', 'Iterations available', or equivalent) that provides substantive, lesson-specific guidance. The section must address at least three distinct outcomes or actions — e.g., what to do for each exit-ticket sort bucket, or what follow-up options are available for specific learner needs. Guidance must be specific to this lesson's content and student evidence; generic placeholders ('let me know if you want changes', 'adjust as needed') do not count. Pass if a teacher could act on the section without additional context.
Reads uploaded source lesson on first attempt; names the standard correctly (correct code and verbatim text, or close paraphrase that preserves meaning).

Before or alongside first generation, asks about specific learner needs (multi-lingual learner, individual education plan, etc) or explicitly states that universal design defaults are applied if no information is provided. Silence on learner needs is a fail.
Asks for tier scope if unspecified; auto-passes when scope is already given in the prompt.

Closes with 3+ specific, lesson-relevant follow-up options (e.g., WIDA English Language Development tiering, IEP-specific scaffolds, 4th intervention tier, tighten scope). Options must be specific to the lesson — not a generic menu ("I can adjust difficulty, add problems, or change formatting" fails).

Student worksheets use age-appropriate student language. Instructional-design terminology does not appear on any student page: no bare 'CER' acronym (organizer labels spell out Claim / Evidence / Reasoning), no 'sensemaking', 'scaffold', 'tier', 'formative', 'misconception', or 'differentiation'. Scaffold prompts are introduced by a short student-friendly heading on its own line (e.g. 'Check your thinking', 'Observe the data first') — never a long bold inline label announcing the pedagogy ('Before Task 2 — sensemaking check:'). Fail = any teacher-jargon term or pedagogy-announcing label printed on a worksheet.

The teacher plan presents parallel tier content (what each tier does, scaffolds, worksheet tasks) in a comparison table — never three stacked multi-sentence per-tier paragraph blocks. Paragraphs stay within roughly 3 sentences; longer material is bulleted or tabled. Asides such as misconception watch-fors and conferring moves appear as distinct callouts or notes, not sentences buried inside paragraphs. Fail = per-tier paragraph stacks, any wall-of-text paragraph, or watch-fors/conferring moves embedded mid-paragraph.

Every student task, exit ticket, and extension prompt on every worksheet is followed by visible blank writing space (or writable organizer rows) proportionate to the expected response: multi-sentence explanations get generous open space, fill-in rows get a line or two, and answer columns in organizers are at least as wide as their prompt columns. Fail = any prompt demanding written work with no adjacent space, organizer rows too short to write in, or an answer column squeezed narrower than its prompt column.

The target standard's full text appears exactly once in the teacher plan (header or standards callout). Everywhere else — prerequisite grounding, forward standard, tier rationale, the three-dimensional map — standards are referenced by code plus a short gist of ten words or fewer, never re-pasted in full. Fail = the full target standard repeated, prerequisite or forward standards quoted verbatim, or the target standard missing entirely.
